item.title: 我用人工智能来对抗我女朋友的脑瘤-自由媒体
item.description:
Amy had just turned 25, but her body seemed to be turning against her: crushing fatigue, bone density loss, and months without getting her period. We saw multiple doctors. They suggested allergies, burnout, or that maybe she just needed more sleep. Then we got her MRI results back: My girlfriend had a brain tumor.

Amy has a prolactinoma, a tumor of the pituitary gland, the pea-size organ at the base of the brain that controls your hormones. In many cases, prolactinomas are benign, slow growing, and shrink with medication. The growth was caught late, gaining mass fast, and sitting in a rare position that threatens her vision. And Amy’s hormone levels were wildly elevated.

We were told that a standard course of treatment—including two surgeries—would get rid of the tumor entirely. It didn’t. The tumor kept coming back. I spent weeks as her full-time nurse, holding her hand through headaches that left her bedridden. She couldn’t bend over, couldn’t blow her nose for fear of a spinal fluid leak. One night, desperate for answers, I started talking to an AI chatbot. It was then that I had a wild thought: I’m going to cure her myself.
item.author: The Free Press

item.title: 牙买加青少年首次在美国医院接受远程肾脏移植-牙买加观察家
item.description:
A Jamaican teenager facing Stage 5 kidney failure has successfully received a living donor transplant in a historic procedure at the Joe DiMaggio Children’s Hospital in Florida, United States.

Arianna Crockett, an 18-year-old who was born in the United States but raised in Jamaica, received her new kidney Wednesday night, after the organ had been removed from a living donor across the United States earlier that day and flown to South Florida where the teen currently resides.

In the process, she became the first patient ever at Joe DiMaggio Children’s Hospital to receive a kidney from a remotely located living donor.

The donor was Sara Goodall, the 40-year-old wife of Crockett’s cousin, according to an article by the South Florida Sun-Sentinel.

Crockett said she was both nervous and excited during her final dialysis session a few hours before her transplant, the article stated.

“It’s very surreal, and nerve-wracking. So I’m nervous-cited, nervous and excited,” Crockett, who reportedly had been receiving dialysis three times a week after going into renal failure in January, told the news service.

Goodall, too, described herself as “nervous-cited” ahead of the procedure, the South Florida Sun-Sentinel reported, noting that the mother said she wished to give “Ari” the opportunity and that she admires the teen’s positive outlook on life.

The Jamaica Observer reported last year on Crockett’s desperate need for a kidney transplant and her mother, Tracy Evans’ plea for a living donor to save her daughter’s life.

READ: Arianna needs a living kidney donor

“We remain strong and trust God that there is a kidney for her,” the mother said at the time.

Crockett was diagnosed with kidney disease at age six.

She attended Victory Academy in Jamaica up to the ninth grade and about three years ago migrated to South Florida where she finished high school.
item.author: Jamaica Observer

item.title: 可逆男性避孕方法刚刚朝着成为现实迈出了一大步- Earth.com
item.description:
For decades, researchers have chased what many call the holy grail of male birth control: something that’s nonhormonal, reversible, long-lasting, and highly reliable – without messing with a man’s overall health.

A new proof-of-principle study from Cornell University suggests one promising route: temporarily shutting down sperm production by targeting a key checkpoint in meiosis, the cell division process that produces sex cells.

The work was focused on mice, but it lays out an important idea: if you interrupt meiosis at the right moment, you can stop sperm production and then allow it to restart later, with fertility returning.

“Our study shows that mostly we recover normal meiosis and complete sperm function, and more importantly, that the offspring are completely normal,” said Paula Cohen, director of the Cornell Reproductive Sciences Center.

Men have limited birth control options

Right now, male contraception is basically condoms or vasectomies. Condoms are effective when used consistently and correctly, but they’re not long-acting.

Vasectomies offer a long-term option, but many men hesitate because they require surgery, and doctors can’t guarantee reversal, even though they can sometimes perform it.

On the hormonal side, researchers have been cautious. Hormonal birth control can work, but shifting hormones can also come with side effects and the history of hormonal contraception has made scientists wary of repeating problems or introducing new risks.

So Cornell’s team took a different approach: don’t change hormones, but change the sperm pipeline directly.

Protecting fertility and stopping sperm

The researchers focused on meiosis because it’s a natural “checkpoint-heavy” process. Importantly, they wanted to stop sperm production without damaging the spermatogonial stem cells – the foundational cells that keep sperm production possible over a lifetime.

“We didn’t want to impact the spermatogonial stem cells, because if you kill those, a man will never become fertile again,” Cohen said.

They also didn’t want to interfere too late in the process, because once developing sperm enter later stages, there’s a risk that some viable sperm could still slip through.

So researchers targeted meiosis as a middle ground, where the body actively produces sperm but can potentially pause the system without permanently shutting it down.

The molecule used to pause sperm

To test the concept, the team used JQ1, a small-molecule inhibitor originally created as a research tool in cancer and inflammatory disease studies.

Researchers are not pitching JQ1 as the final contraceptive product. It has neurological side effects that make it unsuitable as a real-world medication in its current form.

But researchers know it disrupts a specific stage of meiosis called prophase I, which made it useful as a “test case” to see if this approach could work.

JQ1 interferes by killing cells during prophase I and also blocking the gene expression needed for later sperm development.

What happened when sperm stopped

The researchers gave male mice JQ1 for three weeks. During that period, sperm production shut down completely.

They found the mice produced no sperm, and meiosis showed clear disruption at the molecular level, including changes in chromosome behavior during prophase I.

Then they stopped the drug and watched what happened next. Within about six weeks, most of the healthy signs of prophase I returned, and sperm production restarted.

And when the researchers bred the mice, the males were fertile again. They also followed the next generation and found the pups were healthy and later fertile as well.

“It shows that we recover complete meiosis, complete sperm function and, more importantly, that the offspring are completely normal,” Cohen said.

What this could mean for men

This isn’t a consumer-ready contraceptive yet. It’s an early step that shows the logic can work: target a meiosis checkpoint, pause sperm production, then let it recover.

Cohen suggested that if this approach becomes a real product, it could deliver a long-acting injection every few months or possibly a patch, helping users stay consistent and maintain reliability.

The headline here isn’t “JQ1 is the new male birth control.” Cornell’s study strengthens the idea that scientists can target meiosis itself safely and reversibly.

This opens the door to designing better, safer molecules that do the same job without the side effects.

If researchers achieve this, they could shift male contraception away from temporary (condoms) or semi-permanent (vasectomy) options toward a reliable, reversible, private, and long-acting solution.

The study is published in the Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences.

—–

Like what you read? Subscribe to our newsletter for engaging articles, exclusive content, and the latest updates.

Check us out on EarthSnap, a free app brought to you by Eric Ralls and Earth.com.

—–
item.author: Earth.com

item.title: 在博伊西机场接触麻疹？爱达荷州卫生官员发出警告-爱达荷州政治家
item.description:
Health officials are warning travelers of a possible measles exposure at the Boise Airport.

The Idaho Department of Health and Welfare said in a news release Thursday that a person with the highly contagious virus traveled through the airport early in the morning on Sunday, March 29, before 7:40.

Idahoans who shared flights with the person are being notified, the release said.

Measles is a virus that spreads through the air when an infected person coughs, sneezes or talks. Those who aren’t vaccinated can become infected up to two hours after a person with the virus leaves the same room or area, health officials said.

People who may have been exposed should watch for symptoms such as fever, runny nose, cough and red, watery eyes, followed by a rash. In some cases, measles can lead to pneumonia, brain infection and death.

The warning comes as many countries, including popular tourist destinations and parts of the U.S., are experiencing measles outbreaks, the release said.

Health and Welfare encouraged those planning to travel, especially by air, to talk with a doctor or other health care provider beforehand about vaccinations.

The measles, mumps and rubella (MMR) vaccine is highly effective at preventing measles and is recommended for children 12 to 15 months old, with a second dose at 4 to 6 years old, the release said.

Infants younger than 12 months old are generally not recommended to get the vaccine, though infants 6 to 11 months old are recommended to get a dose before traveling internationally.

Idaho has one of the worst measles vaccination rates in the U.S.
